title: Multi-Factor Authentication
description: Test your understanding of MFA, stronger MFA methods, and safe responses to suspicious login prompts.

choices.text: A way to reuse one password across all accounts
A security measure that requires more than one form of verification
A method for disabling login alerts
A tool that removes the need for passwords entirely

questions.explanation: MFA requires more than one form of verification, such as a password plus a code or approval prompt.

questions.prompt: True or False: MFA can help protect an account even if a password is stolen.
questions.explanation: MFA adds another layer of protection, so a stolen password alone may not be enough for an attacker to log in.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a common MFA method?
questions.explanation: Authenticator apps are a common MFA method that generate temporary login codes.

questions.prompt: Which MFA method is generally stronger than SMS-based codes?
questions.explanation: Authenticator apps are generally considered stronger than SMS because text messages can sometimes be intercepted or redirected.

questions.prompt: Which option is considered one of the strongest MFA methods?
questions.explanation: Hardware security keys provide one of the strongest forms of MFA protection.

questions.prompt: What should you do if you receive an MFA prompt you did not expect?

choices.text: Approve it quickly to clear the notification
Ignore it forever and tell no one
Do not approve it and report it according to policy
Approve it if it happens more than once

questions.explanation: Unexpected MFA prompts may mean someone is trying to access your account. They should not be approved.

questions.prompt: What is a push fatigue attack?

choices.text: A slow internet connection during login
Repeated MFA prompts sent in hopes that a user approves one
A software update for mobile devices

questions.explanation: Push fatigue attacks rely on repeated prompts to annoy or confuse a user into approving a malicious login request.

questions.prompt: True or False: It is acceptable to share your MFA code with someone claiming to be IT if the request sounds urgent.
questions.explanation: MFA codes should never be shared in response to an unverified request, even if the person sounds urgent or official.

questions.prompt: Why do organizations use MFA for sensitive systems?

choices.text: Because passwords alone may not be enough
Because it removes all login risk forever
Because it replaces the need for security awareness
Because it makes password reuse safe

questions.explanation: Organizations use MFA because passwords can be stolen, guessed, or reused, and MFA adds another layer of defense.

questions.prompt: If something seems wrong with your MFA activity, what is the best response?

choices.text: Approve the request and change nothing
Do not approve it, change your password if needed, and report it

questions.explanation: The safest response is to deny unexpected prompts, secure the account, and report the issue through the proper process.
